title: Recursion: Functions That Call Themselves
subtitle: Strange at first, beautiful once it clicks

sections.heading: The wild idea
sections.body: A **recursive function** is a function that calls itself. That sounds insane — how does it ever stop? The trick: every recursive function needs a **base case**, a condition that returns *without* recursing. Otherwise it loops forever.

sections.title: Russian nesting dolls
sections.body: You open a doll. Inside is a smaller doll. You open that. Inside is an even smaller doll. Eventually you reach the tiniest doll — that's solid, no more to open. That smallest doll is the base case. Each doll you opened along the way trusted that the next one would handle the rest.

steps.text: factorial(1) hits the base case and returns 1

hints: Base case: when n is 0 or 1, factorial is 1.

questions.question: What MUST every recursive function have?
options: A base case that stops the recursion
questions.explanation: Without a base case, the function calls itself forever and crashes.

questions.question: What does this print?
